\tableofcontents
\section{Introduction}

% Introduction to actor models and their communication mechanism.
Actor models provide a framework for concurrent systems. In these
systems, the main entity is the notion of an \emph{actor}. Actors are
independent computational units that encapsulate the execution environment of
their processing logic. Asynchronous communication between actors occurs
solely via messages. A message is defined as any piece of data sent from one
actor to another. The reception of these messages is handled by the recipient
actor's \emph{mailbox}, a dedicated piece of storage that preserves message
order while remaining separate from the actor's core logic.

% Challenges in actor system message flow management
One challenge in actor systems is managing message flow under high volumes.
Although some techniques such as rate limiting and%
\emph{backpressure routing}~\cite{backpressure} help regulate message rates,
these control mechanisms may also fail under heavily loaded systems. Message
delivery is often assumed to be reliable, but practical implementations
must consider the possibility of message loss and delays from processing
bottlenecks or crashing actors.

% Proposes a solution by decoupling mailboxes from processing actors.
To address some of these challenges, in particular the need to handle message
loss and delays in processing messages, we propose decoupling mailboxes
from \emph{processing actors}, which are the actors that carry out the processing
logic. In this design, communication occurs through message passing to
dedicated \emph{mailbox actors}, with processing actors pulling messages only
when ready to handle them. %
This approach is simple and similar in spirit to Kilim's buffered mailboxes
(multi-producer, single consumer queues) ~\cite{kilim-framework}, allowing us to
give an explicit specification of the message-passing mechanism, where the
mailbox actors remain opaque to both senders and receivers to maintain
compatibility with existing actor frameworks such as Agha's Object-oriented
programming Actor Model~\cite{foundation97agha,taxonomy-of-actor-models}.

% Outlines the benefits of the proposed architectural separation.
The architectural separation of mailboxes from processing actors provides
several key benefits. First, it cleanly separates message management from
processing logic, allowing mailbox actors to specialize in queue management
while processing actors focus on their core functionality. Second, it enables
flexible message scheduling—different mailbox implementations can support
priority queues, batch processing, or custom dispatch strategies without affecting
processing actors. Third, mailbox actors become modular components that can be
modified or replaced independently.

% Discusses the trade-offs and potential drawbacks of the design.
This design may come with some trade-offs. The primary drawback we foresee is
increased system complexity due to additional actors and message-passing
overhead. This manifests as higher resource usage from running more actors and
potential latency increases when mailbox actors run on different nodes than
their processing actors. However, with careful design, these costs can be
outweighed by the benefits of a more flexible system.

% How this paper is organized and what is the contribution.

\paragraph{Contributions}
First, we propose a new architectural
separation between processing actors and their mailboxes in \Cref{sec:model},
which enables improved flexibility and scalability for message management. Second,
we formulate operational semantics in \Cref{sec:semantics} for both the decoupled
architecture and its explicit message-passing mechanism, making the system's
design formally explicit. This description provides sufficient grounding to
implement a library on top of a compatible system/platform such as Erlang, and
to formalise and prove key system properties such as liveness guarantees and
correctness under concurrent message processing.

\section{Notation}\label{sec:notation}

We use an informal type-theoretic notation. For example, a term $x$ of type $A$
is denoted $x : A$. Capital letters or capitalized words are used for types,
and lowercase letters or lowercase words are used for terms. There is a universe
of types $U$, so if $A$ is a type, then $A : U$. We also work with inductive
types presented in the following form:

\begin{align*}
  A := \cdots \mid \mathsf{C}(A_1, \dots, A_n) \, ,
\end{align*}

where $\mathsf{C}$ is a constructor of the type $A$, and $A_1, \dots, A_n$ are
the types of the constructor arguments. The inductive type of natural numbers is
denoted~$\N$. The type $\mathsf{Unit}$ has exactly one term, denoted
$\mathsf{unit}$. The empty type is denoted $\bot$. Another recurrent type is the
type of parametric lists, denoted $\mathsf{List}(A)$. The definition that
term $x$ \emph{is} $y$ is denoted $x :\equiv y$. Definitionally equal terms are
denoted $a \equiv b$. The proposition that $a$ is equal to $b$ is denoted
$a = b$.

Some types are equipped with structure that allows them to behave as sets, and we use some common
set-theoretic notations: the membership relation is denoted $\in$, so
$a \in A$ is a proposition that $a$ is an element of $A$ (do not confuse with
typing $a : A$). For $A$ and $B$, $A \subseteq B$ denotes set inclusion, meaning
every element of $A$ belongs to $B$. $\varnothing$ denotes the empty set.
Union between sets $A$ and $B$ is denoted $A \cup B$. The intersection is
denoted $A \cap B$. The difference between $A$ and $B$ is denoted $A
\setminus B$. $A \times B$ denotes the Cartesian product of $A$ and $B$, and
their terms are denoted $\langle a,b \rangle$, where $a : A$ and $b : B$. A
\emph{tuple} $\langle a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n \rangle$ is a generalisation of an
ordered pair to an ordered $n$-tuple, where $n \in \N$, of type $A_1 \times
\cdots \times A_n$, and $a_i$ is of type $A_i$ for all $i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$.
If the tuple components are named using sans-serif letters, these names are used
as projections, otherwise, the projections are $\pi_i(a) :\equiv a_i$ for all $i
\in \{1, \dots, n\}$. For example, if $x :\equiv \langle \mathsf{fst},
\mathsf{snd} \rangle$ denotes a tuple with components values can be projected
using $\mathsf{fst}$ and $\mathsf{snd}$ via $\mathsf{fst}(x)$ and
$\mathsf{snd}(x)$, respectively. $A_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus A_n$
denotes the disjoint union of $A_1, \dots, A_n$, and $\mathsf{inj_i}(a_i)$ are
the corresponding injections into $A_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus A_n$ from $A_i$ for
all $i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$. Given a type $A$, we can define the optional type
$\Option{A}$ as $A \oplus \mathsf{Unit}$.
The terms
$\mathsf{none}$ and $\mathsf{some}(a)$ are the corresponding terms for the
optional type when $a : A$. Finally, a function $f$ that takes an argument $a$
of type $A$ and returns a value of type $B$ is denoted $f : A \to B$. We also
use the notation $f[a \mapsto c]$ to define a new function that is similar to $f$
except that the output of $f$ is replaced by $c$ when the input is $a$, if $a$ is
in the domain of $f$. Otherwise, we extend $f$ accordingly. We overload the
notation $\mathcal{N}[a \mapsto c]$ to also apply to sets, so if $\mathcal{N}$ is a
set, then $\mathcal{N}[a \mapsto c]$ is the set resulting from $\mathcal{N}$ by
replacing the element $a$ with $c$, if $a$ is a valid element of $\mathcal{N}$.
Otherwise, we extend $\mathcal{N}$ accordingly.


\section{Model}\label{sec:model}

This section presents a model based on the Engine Model \cite{engine-art}
without any time component. In this actor-like model, the system consists of
\emph{engines}, which are similar to the notion of actor in Agha's Actor Model
\cite{foundation97agha} but are equipped with more context.

An engine is comprised of three main components: a \emph{configuration}, an
\emph{execution environment}, and a \emph{behaviour}. The configuration
includes, among other things, the engine's name, location within the system, and
an optional reference to the parent engine. The execution environment includes
an \emph{address book} of other engines to communicate with, including the
engine's own address. Finally, the behaviour defines how the engine reacts to
messages that follow the engine's \emph{message interface}, which is simply a
set of message types that the engine can process. The engine's behaviour is a
set of \emph{guarded actions} consisting of pairs of pre-conditions and actions
to execute. When an engine receives a message, it checks which of its guarded
actions have satisfied pre-conditions, and executes the corresponding action.

\begin{remark}
  The way engine behaviour is defined is similar to the Event-B formalism in
  that event-driven system transitions are specified by a set of pre-conditions
  on sets that form the current system state and the actions to execute if those
  pre-conditions are satisfied. The actions are defined using a language derived
  from process calculi, which often include operations such as parallel composition,
  a null operation, sequencing, nondeterministic choice, and recursion.
  %Here, we have a more restricted set of actions, as will be seen in \Cref{def:effects}.
\end{remark}

The model assumes an ambient \emph{system} $\kappa$ mainly consisting of a set of
\emph{nodes}~$\mathcal{N}$, a set of \emph{engines} $\mathcal{E}$, following a
specific \emph{message-passing mechanism} (MPM for short) that will be described
later, among other components.

Engines are distributed across the nodes of the system, with each engine being
associated with a unique node. Each entity in the system has a unique identifier,
allowing nodes and engines to be denoted with $n_i$ and $e_j$ respectively, where
$i, j \in \N$.

Following the message-passing paradigm, the MPM enforces policies for message
delivery and flow control. This mechanism operates on a set of \emph{messages}
which we denote with $\mu$. Each message contains information about its
sender and receiver.

To reason about the system, we use a state machine that evolves in discrete
steps. So, there is a notion of \say{previous} and \say{next} for each entity
with respect to the system, including the set of messages $\mu$. The steps are
denoted using superscripts, so $\kappa^i$ is the $i$-th step of the system, and
$\mu^i$ is the set of messages in the $i$-th step.

% Within the system, each entity has a unique identifier. However, engines can also be
% referenced by name rather than by address. Engine names provide a more legible way
% to refer to engines in the system, making them easier to work with. Since names
% are unique and unambiguous, they can be used interchangeably with addresses when
% referring to engines.


\subsection{Addresses and names}\label{sec:addresses-and-names}

\begin{definition}\label{def:address}

An \textbf{address} for an engine $e_j$ is a tuple $a \equiv \langle
\mathsf{node}, \mathsf{id} \rangle$, where $\mathsf{node}(a)$ is the node
identifier where the engine runs, and $\mathsf{id}(a)$ is the engine's unique
identifier.
\end{definition}

\begin{axiom}\label{ax:address}
Each engine is uniquely identified by an \emph{address} in the system.
\end{axiom}


\subsection{Message structure}\label{sec:message-structure}

Engine communication occurs through typed messages.
\begin{definition}[Message]\label{def:messages}
A message is a triple $m \equiv \langle s,\, t,\, v \rangle$ where:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $s$ is the sender address (source)
    \item $t$ is the target address (destination)
    \item $v$ is a payload of type $\mathsf{Msg_j}$ corresponding to the message interface of target engine $e_j$
\end{itemize}
We define projection functions $\mathsf{sender}(m)$, $\mathsf{target}(m)$, and $\mathsf{msg}(m)$ to extract the respective components of a message.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Message Interface]\label{def:message-interface}%
The \textbf{message interface} of an engine $e_j$ is the sum type
$\mathsf{Msg_j}$ where the type constructors address the different message types
that the engine can process.
\end{definition}

To refer to an arbitrary message type \emph{within the system}, we use the type
$\mathsf{Msg}$, the sum type of all message interfaces, and therefore the valid
message types, that is, $\mathsf{Msg} :\equiv \bigoplus_{j} \mathsf{Msg_j}$
where $j$ ranges over all engines in the system. To simplify the notation, we
assume message type subtyping, that is, $\mathsf{Msg_j} \subseteq \mathsf{Msg}$
for all $j \in \mathcal{E}$.

\subsection{Engine components}\label{sec:engine-components}

An engine is a tuple of components which may contain immutable data such as
their name and parent. Although, other information such as its location, status,
and environment may change during the engine's lifecycle.
% YEs, we should allow engines to move around the system.

\begin{definition}[Engine Lifecyle]\label{def:engine-status}
The \textbf{status} of an engine $e_j$, of type $\mathsf{EngineStatus}$, determines
the engine's operational mode and thus its lifecycle.
\begin{equation}\label{eq:engine-status}
\mathsf{EngineStatus} := \mathsf{ready} \mid \mathsf{busy} \mid \mathsf{terminated}  \, .
\end{equation}
% tikz
\begin{figure}%[!hbp]
\centering
\[\begin{tikzcd}
	& {\mathsf{ready}} \\
	{\mathsf{terminated}} && {\mathsf{busy}}
	\arrow[from=1-2, to=2-1]
	\arrow[from=1-2, to=2-3, bend right]
	\arrow[from=2-3, to=1-2, bend right]
	\arrow[from=2-3, to=2-1, bend left]
\end{tikzcd}\]
\caption{An engine life cycle. Every engine starts in a
\textsf{ready} state waiting for a message, and once it reacts to a message it
changes to \textsf{busy}. Then, depending on its logic, the engine may either
return to \textsf{ready} or \textsf{terminated}.}
\label{fig:engine-status}
\end{figure}

\end{definition}

As illustrated in \Cref{fig:engine-status}, an engine is said to be
\textsf{ready} if it can process a message. Being \textsf{busy}
means the engine is currently processing a message. And \textsf{terminated}
means the engine will not process any more messages. In such a case, the system
will remove the engine in the next \emph{step}, and disregard any messages sent
to it.

To operate, an engine needs a \emph{configuration} and an \emph{environment}.
In the configuration, we find what cannot be changed, such as the engine's name,
location, parent, and \emph{the operational mode}. So, let's define first what
the operational mode is about.

\begin{definition}\label{def:engine-mode}
The \textbf{operational mode} of an engine $e_j$, of type $\mathsf{EngineMode}$,
determines the engine's operational mode, a tag used by the system to
distinguish the purpose of the engine.
\begin{equation}\label{eq:engine-mode}
\mathsf{EngineMode} := \mathsf{process} \mid \mathsf{mail} \, .
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

As we will see later, the operational mode of an engine determines how the
system routes messages to appropriate engines. Those configured as mailboxes
receive special treatment within the message-passing mechanism. Since the
operational mode becomes immutable once an engine is created, this critical
attribute goes into the engine's configuration.

\begin{definition}\label{def:engine-configuration} The \textbf{configuration} of
an engine $e_j$ often denoted by $\mathsf{cfg}_j$ is a tuple $\langle
\mathsf{name}, \mathsf{parent} , \mathsf{mode} \rangle$ of type $\mathsf{Cfg}$.
The name of the engine is its identifier, and \textsf{parent} is an optional
reference (address) of the engine's parent. The third component, the $\mathsf{mode}$, is the engine's
operational mode as in \Cref{def:engine-mode}.
\end{definition}

Recall that engines are isolated from each other and process messages
independently. However, an engine's configuration alone is insufficient for
proper operation since some form of state is required. Therefore, each engine
encapsulates its own state as part of its execution context, which we refer to
as the engine's \emph{environment}.

\begin{definition}\label{def:engine-environment}

Given a type $S$ representing the local state of an engine $e_j$, an execution
\textbf{environment} of $e_j$ is a tuple $s \equiv\langle \mathsf{state},
\mathsf{addrbook} \rangle$ of type $\mathsf{Env}(S)$, where $\mathsf{state}(s)$
is of type $S$, and $\mathsf{addrbook}(s)$ is the engine's address book, a
non-empty list containing the names of other engines, including its own.
$\mathsf{addrbook}(s)$ is of type $\mathsf{List}(\mathsf{Addr})$.
\end{definition}

\subsection{Execution directives}\label{sec:execution-directives}

The behaviour of an engine is a declarative description of what it does when it
reacts to a message. This description of computation is via an effect system
consisting of commands to the system to be executed at runtime.

\begin{definition}[Effect]\label{def:effects}%
Given a type $S$ representing the local state of an engine $e_j$, an
\textbf{effect} is of type $\mathsf{Effect}(S)$, which considers all the
possible ways an engine can influence the system by reacting to a message.

\begin{equation}\label{eq:effects}
\begin{aligned} %TODO: review this grammar
\mathsf{Effect}(S) := &\quad \mathsf{send}(\mathsf{Addr}, \mathsf{Msg}) & &\text{(message dispatch)} \\
\mid & \quad \mathsf{update}(\mathsf{Env}(S)) & &\text{(engine state modification)} \\
\mid & \quad \mathsf{spawn}(\mathsf{Cfg}_j, \mathsf{Env}(S)) & &\text{(engine creation)} \\
\mid & \quad \mathsf{chain}(\mathsf{Effect}(S), \mathsf{Effect}(S_j)) & &\text{(action sequencing)} \\
\mid & \quad \mathsf{terminate} & &\text{(engine shutdown)} \\
\mid & \quad \mathsf{noop} & &\text{(null operation)\, .}
\end{aligned}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

% TODO: mention how this induces a monad.
% also mention CCS?
\begin{figure}%[!htbp]
% https://q.uiver.app/#
\[\begin{tikzcd}
	{} & {(m, \mathsf{cfg_j},\mathsf{env}_j)} & {} \\
	\\
	{r_1} & \cdots & {r_k} \\
	{e_1:\mathsf{Effect}(S_j)} & \cdots & {e_k:\mathsf{Effect}(S_j)}
	\arrow[curve={height=24pt}, shorten <=24pt, shorten >=24pt, dashed, no head, from=1-1, to=1-3]
	\arrow["{g_1}"', maps to, from=1-2, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{g_i}"', maps to, from=1-2, to=3-2]
	\arrow["{g_k}", maps to, from=1-2, to=3-3]
	\arrow["{c_1}"', maps to, from=3-1, to=4-1]
	\arrow[maps to, from=3-2, to=4-2]
	\arrow["{c_k}", maps to, from=3-3, to=4-3]
\end{tikzcd}\]%
\caption{For an engine $e_j$, its behaviour acts on a message $m$ of type
$\mathsf{Msg}_j$ and an engine's configuration and environment $\mathsf{cfg}_j$
and $\mathsf{env}_j$, respectively. The pre-conditions, denoted as $g_i$, are
guards that determine which action is executed. The symbols $r_i$ represent
output that feeds into the actions $c_i$. The actions, denoted as $c_i$, are
effects as in \Cref{def:effects} that are executed if their pre-conditions are
satisfied. If no pre-conditions are satisfied, the action is a null operation,
$\mathsf{noop}$.}
\label{fig:effect}
\end{figure}

Given an engine $e_j$, the following definition specifies the guard and action
components of an engine's behaviour.

\begin{definition}[Guard]\label{def:guards}%
A \textbf{guard} is a pre-condition that, when an engine reacts to a message
with its environment $\mathsf{env}_j$ and configuration $\mathsf{cfg}_j$,
returns $\mathsf{none}$ if the guard is not satisfied, and returns data
otherwise. Therefore, a guard can be seen as a mapping of type $\mathsf{Msg}
\times \mathsf{Cfg}_j \times \mathsf{Env}_j(S_j) \to \Option{R}$ for some type
$R$, where $R$ is the specific type of data returned when the guard is
satisfied.
\end{definition}

On the computational aspects of an engine, the behaviour evaluating the
different guards, and for each guard, the action to be executed, whenever the
guard is satisfied, is as follows:

\begin{definition}\label{def:action}%
Given an engine $e_j$, an \textbf{action} acting on data of type $R$ is a
mapping of type $R \times \mathsf{Msg}_j \times \mathsf{Cfg}_j \times
\mathsf{Env}_j(S_j) \to \mathsf{Effect}(S_j)$.
\end{definition}

Then, we compose a guard and an action to form a \emph{guarded action}, which is
the building block of which an engine's behaviour consists of. The separation of
guard and action allows for a more modular design. Consider that one may want to
reuse the guard of an engine in different engines or decouple the logic of the
guard against the action, allowing the pre-conditions to be reused in scenarios
where the action is not applicable.

\begin{definition}\label{def:guarded-action}%
  Given an engine $e_j$, a \textbf{guarded action} of the engine's behaviour is
  a tuple $\langle g_j, a_j \rangle$, where:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item $g_j$ is a guard as in \Cref{def:guards} that determines when the
    action $a_j$ should execute, and
    \item $a_j$ is an action of type $\mathsf{Effect}(S_j)$ as in
    \Cref{def:effects} that specifies what to execute
  \end{itemize}
\end{definition}

Together, both the guard and action induce a mapping of type

\begin{equation*}\label{eq:guarded-action}%
\mathsf{Msg} \times \mathsf{Cfg}_j
\times \mathsf{Env}_j(S_j) \to \mathsf{Effect}(S_j)\,.
\end{equation*}
This mapping takes in a message, engine configuration, and environment, and
outputs the effect to execute when the guard is satisfied.

So, upon receiving a message, an engine $e_j$ checks which of its guards is
satisfied, and if so, the engine executes the corresponding action, as
illustrated in \Cref{fig:effect}.

\begin{remark}\label{rem:turing-completeness}%
A question at this point is whether engines can enable unbounded computation.
Engines' behaviours as introduced in~\cite{engine-art} are Turing complete. To
see this, we need to show that the design satisfies the two fundamental
requirements: conditional branching through guard-state interactions and
indefinite looping via message-passing recursion. Note that the message-passing
mechanism says nothing about forbidding engines from sending messages to
themselves, and this is a powerful mechanism that allows for recursive patterns.
So, while individual effects as listed in \Cref{def:effects} lack explicit
looping constructs, and it may not seem at first glance there is a recursive
mechanism, the combination of guarded actions acting as conditional statements
with open-ended message passing enables computational universality.
\end{remark}

\subsection{Engines}\label{sec:processing-engines}

Having defined the components that make up an engine's behaviour, we can now
formally specify engines themselves. As the fundamental computational units of
our system, engines encapsulate both the processing logic and state management
capabilities. We have established the types of their constituent parts, that is,
guards, actions, configurations and environments. 

\begin{definition}\label{def:engine}
An \textbf{engine} is the computational unit of the system, formally specified by a tuple
$\langle i, b, \mathsf{status}, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle$ where:
\begin{itemize}
\item $i$ is the engine's message interface, a type as described in \Cref{def:message-interface},
\item $b$ is the engine's behaviour ---a set of guarded actions as defined in \Cref{def:guarded-action},
\item $\mathsf{status}$ is the engine's status flag as specified in \Cref{def:engine-status},
\item $\mathsf{cfg}$ is the engine's configuration as described in
\Cref{def:engine-configuration}, and
\item $\mathsf{env}$ is the engine's execution environment as outlined in \Cref{def:engine-environment}.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

% \begin{remark}\label{rem:engine-init}
% No need to initialize an engine. Once declared, an engine is ready to process
% messages.
% \end{remark}

Our system defines two engine types: \emph{processing engines} and \emph{mailbox
engines}. Users can only explicitly create processing engines, while mailbox
engines are automatically created by the system when a processing engine is
instantiated, as detailed in \Cref{sec:creation-rules}. A processing engine is
distinguished by having its operational mode set to $\mathsf{process}$ through
its configuration's mode field. The internals of mailbox engines are described
in \Cref{def:mailbox-engine}.

\begin{definition}\label{def:mailbox-engine}
A \emph{mailbox engine} $m_j$ is a system-configured companion engine
automatically created when instantiating a processing engine $e_j$. It must
satisfy:
\begin{itemize}
\item Operational mode set to $\mathsf{mail}$ (per \Cref{def:engine-mode})
\item Parent reference pointing to its paired processing engine $e_j$
\item Fixed message interface $i_\mathsf{mbox}$ with two operation types:
\begin{equation*}
\begin{array}{rcl}
  i_\mathsf{mbox} &:= &\mathsf{append}(\mathsf{Msg_j}) \\
                  &|  &\mathsf{pull}(\mathsf{Msg_j})
\end{array}
\end{equation*}
both carrying payloads of type $\mathsf{Msg_j}$
\item State $q$ of type $S$ implementing a queue-like structure with:
  \begin{itemize}
  \item $\emptyset$ denoting empty state,
  \item message insertion to support $\mathsf{append}$ messages, $\mathsf{append}(q, v) : S \times \mathsf{Msg_j} \to S$, and
  \item message retrieval to support $\mathsf{pull}$ messages, $\mathsf{pull}(q) : S \to \mathsf{Msg_j} \times S$
  \end{itemize}
\item Behaviour enforcing semantics: $\mathsf{append}$ operations always precede
$\mathsf{pull}$ operations in message processing
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

With mailbox engines added to the system, we have a strict producer-consumer
pattern introduced through explicit pull semantics, decoupling message storage
from processing logic. This allows for independent scaling of message ingestion
and core computation.
%TODO: add a reference to the corresponing rules in the operational semantics

\subsection{Nodes}\label{sec:nodes}

Having previously introduced the system $\kappa$, which comprises a set of nodes
$\mathcal{N}$ that host engines $\mathcal{E}$ governed by a message-passing
mechanism defined by the system's requirements, we now formally define what
constitutes a node.

\begin{definition}\label{def:node}
A \textbf{node} $n_i$ in $\mathcal{N}$ is a tuple
$\langle \mathsf{id}, \mathcal{E}_i, \omega_i \rangle$ where $\mathsf{id}$
corresponds to the node's unique identifier across the system, $\mathcal{E}_i$
is the set of engines running on the node, and $\omega_i$ represents this specific
node's operations.
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}\label{rem:node-functions}
For convenience, we derive the following functions for any node $n_i$:
\begin{itemize}
\item $\alpha_i : \mathcal{E}_i \to \mathsf{Addr}$ is a total mapping that
returns the address of any engine living on node $n_i$.
\item The image of $\alpha_i$ is $\mathsf{Addr}_i :\equiv \alpha_i(\mathcal{E}_i)$.
\item $\epsilon_i : \mathsf{Addr}_i \to \mathcal{E}_i$ is a mapping that, given
an address in the node, that is, $\langle n_i, \mathsf{id}_j \rangle$ in
$\mathsf{Addr}_i$, returns the corresponding engine $e_j \in \mathcal{E}_i$.
\end{itemize}
\end{remark}
\begin{remark}\label{rem:node-operations}
By design, nodes cannot create, destroy, or modify engines directly. These
operations require global coordination through system-level mechanisms rather
than node-local decisions. This architectural constraint exists because nodes
lack awareness of critical system-wide concerns like address registry management,
topology relationships between components, and coordination mechanisms for
distributed state consistency.
\end{remark}

\subsection{System state}\label{sec:state}

Having defined nodes and their capabilities, we now define the overall system
state. A system consists of a collection of nodes and a set of in-transit
messages that represent ongoing communication between engines. Like any state
machine, we must precisely define the system state before specifying the
transition rules that govern its evolution.

\begin{definition}\label{def:state} A \textbf{state} of the system
$\kappa$ is a tuple $\kappa^{i} \equiv \langle \mathcal{N}^i, \mu^i, \omega^i
\rangle$ where,
\begin{itemize}
\item $\mathcal{N}^i$ is the set of nodes,
\item $\mu^i$ is the set of in-transit messages representing ongoing communications, and
\item $\omega^i$ represents the system capabilities and operations.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

% TODO: what are the implications that  \mu^i is a set?
% for the append rule, we require to (i) access a message in the set, and (ii)
% remove it from the set. However, it's not clear if we should preserve the order
% of the messages in the set. If needed, a timestamp could be added to the messages
% to preserve the order.

\begin{remark}\label{rem:system-time-notation} The superscript $i$ in $\kappa^i$
denotes an instantiation of the system. This can be viewed as a snapshot of the
system at a point in time denoted by index $i$, with the ability to evolve to a
successor state $\kappa^{i+1}$.
\end{remark}

The \emph{initial state} of the system is denoted by $\kappa^0$. In principle, we could
define the initial state as $\kappa^0 \equiv \langle \emptyset, \emptyset,
\emptyset \rangle$, no nodes, no messages, and no capabilities.
Via primitives that we will define later, we can step from the initial state to a
successor state with nodes, messages, and capabilities.

\begin{remark}\label{rem:system-model} The Engine Model \cite{engine-art}, which
inspires the system described here, is a communication-based system model
incorporating temporal aspects and dynamic process creation. Here we retain only
the dynamic process creation capability, leaving temporal considerations for
future work. Like \textsc{DETCs}, we use labelled transition system semantics
where transitions occur when processes react to messages by updating their
state, generating new messages and engines. The associated system in
\textsc{DETC} is defined in terms of active processes and a set of messages $M$,
assuming all messages eventually get delivered. A similar approach is adopted
here for the state in \cref{def:state}, and we assume all messages are
eventually delivered as stated in the axiom below.
\end{remark}

\begin{axiom}\label{ax:message-delivery}
For any engines $e, e' \in \mathcal{E}$ and message in transit within
system state $\kappa$, there exists a subsequent state $\kappa'$ where $m$ is
eventually delivered to its intended recipient.
\end{axiom}

The system must provide primitives to aid in the creation of different
entities such as nodes and engines. The system provides the following functions:

\begin{itemize}
\item $\mathsf{freshId} : \mathsf{Unit} \to \mathsf{Addr}$ is the function that
generates a fresh identifier for node creation.
\item $\mathsf{freshName} : \mathsf{Unit} \to \mathsf{Name}$ is the function that
generates a fresh name for an engine.
\item $\mathsf{mbox} : \mathsf{Addr} \to \mathsf{Addr}$ is the function that
outputs the address of the dedicated mailbox engine for a given processing
engine.
% \begin{equation*}
% \begin{aligned}
%   &\mathsf{mbox} : \mathsf{Addr} \to \mathsf{Addr}\\
%   &\mathsf{mbox}(\langle n_i, \mathsf{id}_j \rangle) \equiv \langle n_i, \mathsf{id}_j \rangle
% \end{aligned}
% \end{equation*}

\item $\execEffect$ is the function that executes effects as in \Cref{def:effects}
and outputs the new system state after the effect has been executed.
\end{itemize}

We could also extend the node-specific functions defined in
\Cref{rem:node-functions}. Functions like $\alpha_i$ can be used to construct
a global version $\alpha$ that operates over all engines $\mathcal{E}$ in
$\kappa^i$. This allows us to look up addresses for any engine in the system
regardless of which node it runs.

Not described here are the functions for adding, removing, and modifying nodes.
Also, one may want to move engines between nodes. We leave these considerations
for future work.

\section{Operational Semantics}\label{sec:semantics}

The transitions of the associated state machine of the system define both how the
system evolves and how the message-passing mechanism functions.

Let us recall the notation used below. $\mathcal{E}$ is the set of all engines
across all nodes in $\mathcal{N}$, where $\mathcal{N}$ is the set of nodes.
Additionally, $\alpha$ is the global address map and $\mu$ is the set of
in-transit messages. The operational semantics for the system are defined here
via the reduction relation denoted by $(\rightsquigarrow)$, where $\kappa^i
\rightsquigarrow \kappa^{i+1}$ signifies that the system state $\kappa^i$
evolves to $\kappa^{i+1}$. The notation $\kappa^i$ is mentioned in
\Cref{rem:system-time-notation}.

\subsection{System initialisation and extension rules}\label{sec:creation-rules}

In the following rules, we assume that $\kappa \equiv (\mathcal{N}, \mu,
\omega)$, unless otherwise specified.

\begin{definition}[\textsc{CreateNode}]\label{rule:create-node-ext}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$n \equiv \langle \mathsf{freshId}(\mathsf{unit}), \emptyset, \omega_n \rangle$}
\UnaryInfC{$\kappa \rightsquigarrow (\mathcal{N} \cup \{n\}, \mu, \omega)$}
\end{prooftree}

Where $\omega_n$ contains node-specific operations including:
\begin{itemize}
\item $\alpha_n : \mathcal{E}_n \to \mathsf{Addr}$, node-local address map, and
\item $\epsilon_n : \mathsf{Addr}_n \to \mathcal{E}_n$ for engine lookup given
an engine's address.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

\noindent To create an engine in the system on node $n_i$, we require a
configuration $\mathsf{cfg}$ and an environment $\mathsf{env}$, at least.
Additionally, the new engine must have a registered message interface and an
associated behaviour $b$ that defines how it processes messages.

\begin{definition}[\textsc{CreateEngine}]\label{rule:create-engine}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
% Level 1: Node and configuration setup
\AxiomC{%
$n_i \equiv \langle \mathsf{id}_i, \mathcal{E}_i, \omega_i \rangle \in \mathcal{N}%
% \quad m : \mathsf{ \mathsf{EngineMode}} %
\quad p : \mathsf{Option}(\mathsf{Addr}) %
\quad \mathsf{cfg} \equiv \langle \mathsf{name}, p, \mathsf{process}\rangle$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{
$S : \mathsf{Type} %
\quad \mathsf{env} : \mathsf{Env}(S)  %
\quad in : \mathsf{Msg} %
\quad e_{\mathsf{new}} \equiv \langle in, b,
\mathsf{ready}, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle%
$}
% % Level 3: Engine set and address map updates
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\iota :\equiv \mathsf{freshId}(\mathsf{unit})%
\quad \eta \equiv  \mathsf{freshId}(\mathsf{unit})%
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\mathsf{cfg}_{\mathsf{mbox}} \equiv %
    \langle \mathsf{freshName}(\mathsf{unit}), %
    \iota, %
    \mathsf{mail}%
    \rangle
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
m_{\mathsf{new}} \equiv %
  \langle i_{\mathsf{mbox}}, %
  b_{\mathsf{mbox}}, %
  \mathsf{ready}, %
  \mathsf{cfg}_{\mathsf{mbox}}, %
  e_{\mathsf{mbox}}%
  \rangle
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\alpha_i' \equiv \alpha_i \cup%
\{e_{\mathsf{new}} \mapsto \langle
\mathsf{id}_i, \iota \rangle,
m_{\mathsf{new}} \mapsto \langle
\mathsf{id}_i, \eta \rangle
\}%
\quad \omega_i' \equiv \omega_i[\alpha \mapsto \alpha_i']
$}
\UnaryInfC{$\kappa \rightsquigarrow %
(\mathcal{N}[n_i \mapsto%
  \langle \mathsf{id}_i,%
  \mathcal{E}_i \cup \{e_{\mathsf{new}}, m_{\mathsf{new}}\},%
  \omega_i' \rangle],%
   \mu, %
   \omega[\omega_i \mapsto \omega_i'])$}
\end{prooftree}

The terms $i_{\mathsf{mbox}}$, $b_{\mathsf{mbox}}$, and $e_{\mathsf{mbox}}$
are the message interface, the behaviour, and the environment of the mailbox engine,
respectively, which follow the specification as in \Cref{def:mailbox-engine}.
% TODO: it may seems the function to get mailbox address needs to be defined
% in the node-specific operations.
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}
The rule \textsc{CreateEngine} takes place in the context of a node $n_i$. When defining a
processing engine $e_{\mathsf{new}}$, the system also creates a mailbox engine on the same
node, $m_{\mathsf{new}}$. While the only parameters required by this rule are the
processing engine parameters (configuration and environment) for $e_{\mathsf{new}}$,
one could customize the mailbox parameters, such as its behaviour $b_{\mathsf{mbox}}$,
to enable specialized message routing or priority-based message organisation.
However, for simplicity, we use a uniform mailbox behaviour across all mailbox
engines.
\end{remark}

\begin{comment}
We could think of adding rules for removing nodes and engines. However,
one question just arises to me: how to handle broken references to those
engines that are removed?
\end{comment}

To prevent \emph{zombie} engine instances that could  violate liveness and
safety guarantees, we need to remove terminated engines from the system. This is
done via the \textsc{Clean} rule.

\begin{definition}[\textsc{Clean}]\label{rule:clean}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
e \in \mathcal{E}%
\quad \mathsf{terminated} = \mathsf{status}(e)
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\kappa \rightsquigarrow%
(\mathcal{N} \setminus \{e\},%
\mu,% TODO: Do we NEED to remove references to e???
\omega%
)$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}
Notice how the \textsc{Clean} rule does not remove the engine's mailbox.
Even when removing the processing engine, the mailbox is kept in the system
receiving messages, but not being processed. This ensures that messages are not
lost.
%Also, the mailbox can be reused for future engine instances? as it is a matter
%of changing the engine's configuration.
\end{remark}

\subsection{Message-passing rules}\label{sec:message-passing-rules}

Our message-passing mechanism aims to support (i) asynchronous communication
with non-blocking sends, (ii) mailbox isolation with guaranteed eventual
delivery, and (iii) explicit lifecycle management for engines.

The \textsc{Send} rule governs message communication between engines. When
engine $e_s$ sends a message to engine $e_t$, the rule handles the registration
of the message in $e_t$'s dedicated mailbox. This message must be well-typed
according to $e_t$'s message interface specification. This rule is the only way
to add a message to $\mu$.

\begin{definition}[\textsc{Send}]\label{rule:send}
\hspace{-1.5em}
  \begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
e_s, e_t \in \mathcal{E}%
\quad v : \mathsf{Msg}_t
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
m_t \equiv \mathsf{mbox}(\alpha(e_t))
\quad \mathsf{mail} = \mathsf{mode}(\mathsf{cfg}(\epsilon(m_t)))
$}
\UnaryInfC{$\kappa \rightsquigarrow (\mathcal{N}, \mu \cup%
\{ \langle%
\alpha(e_s),%
m_t,%
\mathsf{append}(v)%
\rangle%
\},%
\omega%
)$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

Now, with a message in-transit, the system needs to deliver it to the target engine's
mailbox. This is done via the \textsc{Enqueue} rule.

\begin{definition}[\textsc{Enqueue}]\label{rule:enqueue}
\hspace{-1.5em}
  \begin{prooftree}
  \AxiomC{$m \equiv \langle s, m_t, \mathsf{append}(v) \rangle \in \mu
  \quad m_t \equiv \mathsf{mbox}(\alpha(e_t))%
  $}
  \noLine
  \UnaryInfC{$%
   \mathsf{mail} = \mathsf{mode}(\mathsf{cfg}(\epsilon(m_t)))%
  \quad q \equiv \mathsf{env}(\epsilon(m_t)) $}
  \UnaryInfC{$\kappa \rightsquigarrow (\mathcal{N}[m_t \mapsto%
  \langle i_{mbox}, b_{mbox}, s_{mbox}, \mathsf{cfg}_{mbox}, q \cup \{v\} \rangle],%
  \mu \setminus \{m\},%
  \omega)$}
  \end{prooftree}
  \end{definition}

  \begin{definition}[\textsc{Dequeue}]\label{rule:dequeue}
  \hspace{-1.5em}
  \begin{prooftree}
  \AxiomC{$e_p \equiv \langle i_p, b_p, \mathsf{ready}, \mathsf{cfg}_p,
  \mathsf{env}_p \rangle
  \quad m_p \equiv \mathsf{mbox}(e_p)
  $}
  \noLine
  \UnaryInfC{$%
  \mathsf{mail} = \mathsf{mode}(\mathsf{cfg}(\epsilon(m_p)))%
  \quad \mathsf{env}(\epsilon(m_p)) = q \cup \{v\}
  $}
  \noLine
  \UnaryInfC{$%
  \mathcal{N}' \equiv
  \mathcal{N}[e_p \mapsto%
  \langle i_p, b_p, \mathsf{busy}, \mathsf{cfg}_p, \mathsf{env}_p \rangle]%
  [m_p \mapsto \langle i_{mbox}, b_{mbox}, s_{mbox}, \mathsf{cfg}_{mbox}, q \rangle]
  $}
  \UnaryInfC{$\kappa \rightsquigarrow (\mathcal{N}', \mu, \omega)$}
  \end{prooftree}
  \end{definition}
After a processing engine retrieves a message via the \textsc{Dequeue} rule, it
enters the \texttt{busy} state to handle the message. This message processing
involves executing the engine's predefined behaviour through the
\textsc{Process} rule, as defined in~\Cref{sec:processing-rules}. However, we
need first to establish how an engine's behaviour evaluates messages through its
guarded actions, and how the evaluation of those effects changes the system state.

\subsection{Behaviour evaluation rules}\label{sec:behavior-eval-rules}

\begin{comment}
restrict attention to machines
whose behaviour is guaranteed to terminate,
\end{comment}

While the system's operational semantics primarily govern inter-engine
communication, individual engines retain full autonomy over their internal
message processing. This subsection formalises the evaluation mechanism for
guarded actions that govern engine responses to messages. Specifically, the
following rules define the process by which an engine $p$ consumes a message $v$
from its mailbox, formalised via the judgment $b_p,
\mathsf{env}_p \vdash v \downarrow E$. This relation indicates that evaluating
message $v$ using behaviour $b_p$ within the context of a busy engine $p$ and its
environment $\mathsf{env}_p$ results in an effect $E$.

\begin{definition}[\textsc{GuardedActionEval}]\label{rule:guarded-action-eval}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
p \in \mathcal{E}%
\quad p \equiv \langle i_p, b_p, \mathsf{busy}, \mathsf{cfg}_p, \mathsf{env}_p \rangle%
\quad v : i_p%
\quad S : \mathsf{Type}%
\quad \mathsf{env}_p : \mathsf{Env}(S)%
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\langle g, a \rangle \in b_p%
\quad g(v, \mathsf{cfg}_p, \mathsf{env}_p) = \mathsf{some}(r)%
\quad E : \mathsf{Effect}(S)%
\quad a(r, v, \mathsf{cfg}_p, \mathsf{env}_p) = E%
$}
\UnaryInfC{$b_p, \mathsf{env}_p \vdash v \downarrow E$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

The \textsc{GuardedActionEval} rule reads as follows. When a guarded action
$\langle g, a \rangle$ exists in behaviour $b_p$ such that: the guard $g$ is
satisfied for message $v$, configuration $\mathsf{cfg}_p$, and environment
$\mathsf{env}_p$, yielding a result $r$; the corresponding action $a$ executed
using $r$, $v$, $\mathsf{cfg}_p$, and $\mathsf{env}_p$ generates effect $E$.

Similarly, if no matching guard is found, the engine will perform a no-op, as
defined in~\Cref{def:effects}.

\begin{definition}[\textsc{NoMatchingGuard}]\label{rule:no-matching-guard}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
p \in \mathcal{E}%
\quad p \equiv \langle i_p, b_p, \mathsf{busy}, \mathsf{cfg}_p, \mathsf{env}_p \rangle%
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$
S : \mathsf{Type}%
\quad \mathsf{env}_p : \mathsf{Env}(S)%
\quad v : i_p%
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\langle g, a \rangle \in b%
\quad g(v, \mathsf{cfg}_p, \mathsf{env}_p) = \mathsf{none}%
$}
\UnaryInfC{$b_p, \mathsf{env}_p \vdash v \downarrow \mathsf{noop}$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\subsection{Effect execution rules}\label{sec:effect-rules}

The function $\execEffect$ executes effects as defined in \Cref{def:effects}.
Its definition is given by case analysis on the different constructors of the
effect data type.

\begin{definition}[\textsc{ESend}]\label{rule:e-send}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$e_s,e_t \in \mathcal{E}%
\quad a_t \equiv \alpha(e_t)%
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
e_t \equiv \langle i_t, b_t, \mathsf{ready}, \mathsf{cfg}_t, \mathsf{env}_t \rangle%
\quad v : i_t%
\quad (\kappa \rightsquigarrow \kappa') \equiv%
 \textsc{Send}(\kappa, e_s, e_t,v)
$}
\UnaryInfC{$\execEffect(\mathsf{send}(a_t, v), \kappa)
\equiv%
\kappa'$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsc{EUpdate}]\label{rule:e-update}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$
e \in \mathcal{E}%
\quad S : \mathsf{Type}%
\quad \mathsf{env}(e) : \mathsf{Env}(S)%
\quad s : \mathsf{Env}(S)%
$}
\UnaryInfC{$\execEffect(\mathsf{update}(s), \kappa) \equiv%
(\mathcal{N}[e \mapsto \langle%
i, b, r, \mathsf{cfg}, s%
 \rangle], \mu, \omega)$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsc{ESpawn}]\label{rule:e-spawn}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
% parent engine
\AxiomC{$%
n_i \in \mathcal{N}%
\quad e \in \mathcal{E}_i%
\quad c \equiv \langle \mathsf{freshName}(\mathsf{unit}),
\alpha(e), \mathsf{process} \rangle%
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
S : \mathsf{Type}%
\quad s : \mathsf{Env}(S)%
\quad (\kappa \rightsquigarrow \kappa') \equiv \textsc{CreateEngine}(\kappa, n_i, c, s)
$}
\UnaryInfC{$\execEffect(\mathsf{spawn}(c, s), \kappa) \equiv \kappa'$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsc{EChain}]\label{rule:e-chain}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$e \in \mathcal{E}_i$}
\AxiomC{$\execEffect(\mathit{eff}_1, e, \kappa) \equiv \kappa'$}
\AxiomC{$\execEffect(\mathit{eff}_2, e, \kappa') \equiv \kappa''$}
\TrinaryInfC{$\execEffect(\mathsf{chain}(\mathit{eff}_1, \mathit{eff}_2),
\kappa)%
\equiv \kappa''$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsc{ETerminate}]\label{rule:e-terminate}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$e \in \mathcal{N} \quad (\kappa \rightsquigarrow \kappa') \equiv%
  \textsc{Clean}(\kappa, e)$}
\UnaryInfC{$\execEffect(\mathsf{terminate}, \kappa) \equiv \kappa'$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsc{ENoop}]\label{rule:e-noop}
  \hspace{-1.5em}
  \begin{prooftree}
  \AxiomC{$\quad e \in \mathcal{E}_i$}
  \UnaryInfC{$\execEffect(\mathsf{noop}, \kappa) \equiv \kappa$}
  \end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

% Finally define effect execution
\begin{definition}[\textsc{EffectExec}]\label{rule:effect-exec}
  \hspace{-1.5em}
  \begin{prooftree}
  \AxiomC{$\execEffect(E, \kappa) = \kappa'$} \UnaryInfC{$\kappa \vdash
  E \rightsquigarrow \kappa'$}
  \end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\subsection{Processing rules}\label{sec:processing-rules}

This section introduces the final and most critical operational rule:
\textsc{Process}, which governs message handling by engines.

\begin{definition}[\textsc{Process}]\label{rule:process}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
n_i \in \mathcal{N}%
\quad p \in \mathcal{E}_i%
\quad p \equiv \langle i_p, b_p, \mathsf{busy}, \mathsf{cfg}_p, \mathsf{env}_p \rangle%
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
m : i_p%
\quad b_p, \mathsf{env}_p \vdash  m \downarrow E%
\quad \kappa \vdash E \rightsquigarrow (\mathcal{N}', \mu', \omega')$}
\UnaryInfC{$\kappa \rightsquigarrow%
\mathcal{N}'[%
\mathcal{E}_i\mapsto \mathcal{E}_i[e_p \mapsto%
\langle i_p, b_p, \mathsf{ready}, \mathsf{cfg}_p, \mathsf{env}_p' \rangle]%
, \mu'
, \omega'
]$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

The formal system established through the set of rules above specifies three key
aspects: (i) message delivery through mailbox operations, (ii) sequential
message processing by engines, and (iii) state transitions through effect
execution. We believe that these core operational rule sets, combined with type
safety guarantees, enable comprehensive analysis of both individual engine
behaviours and overall system dynamics.

The omission of any rule induces system-level inconsistencies or failures:
failure to include \textsc{Enqueue} leaves messages perpetually in-transit;
absence of \textsc{Dequeue} breaks causal message processing relationships; and,
for example, exclusion of \textsc{Clean} results in resource-leaking dormant
engines.

\section{Concluding remarks}\label{sec:related}

The Engine Model~\cite{art-engines}, which serves as our focus within the Actor
model paradigm, shares conceptual ground with Erlang's process-oriented
architecture and similar Actor model implementations that treat actors as
processes~\cite{taxonomy-of-actor-models} ---entities we refer to as
\emph{engines} in our framework.

Fundamentally, engines represent processes augmented with additional structural
components but not particularly crucial to the main design proposed here. Here we propose mailboxes as actors that naturally extends to any Actor-like that shares a process-oriented paradigm. 

\paragraph{Related work}
Kilim's framework~\cite{kilim-framework} aligns to the mailbox decoupling, goal of this paper. Kilim achieves the mailbox separation through
specialised queues. However, Kilim's implementation treats mailboxes as passive
data structures rather than first-class actors. This limitation persists across
most Actor model
implementations like Agha's Classic Actor Model~\cite{foundation97agha}, and SALSA~\cite{taxonomy-of-actor-models}, where
mailboxes typically function as internal components with limited exposed
functionality beyond basic queue operations, or like Erlang, where mailbox
interaction remains a low-level system primitive with opaque handling~\cite{erlang-2010}.

In short, here, by promoting mailboxes to
engines/process, we synthesise Kilim's structural separation with Erlang's
operational transparency while introducing new capabilities via custom mailboxes
behaviour.

Summarising, mailbox engines function as \emph{secretaries},
intelligent message brokers that can be configured to, for example, perform content-based
filtering rules, engine-specific priority-based message
organisation, temporal/delayed delivery, and implementing debugging mechanisms.


\paragraph{Multiple mailboxes per processing actor}
While our architecture could naturally accommodate multiple mailbox actors per
processing actor, a pattern that might enable specialized message routing or
priority queues, we consciously omit this extension from our core design. This
restriction serves to preserve the system's verifiable properties. Concurrent
pulling from multiple mailboxes could introduce race conditions that violate our
exactly-once delivery guarantee. The coordination overhead required for atomic
operations across distributed mailboxes would also compromise the simplicity
benefits of our decoupled architecture. Therefore, we intentionally maintain a
one-to-one relationship between processing actors and their mailboxes, as this
constraint ensures reliable message ordering while preserving the opacity and
encapsulation benefits central to our design.

\paragraph{System properties}
There is nothing that prevents us from proving system properties for this
extended model. A possible starting point would be to add a timeout primitive to
the engine model and demonstrate that the system remains live and makes progress
even in the presence of timeouts for practical implementations.

\paragraph{Practical implementations}
While several systems implement related concepts, no existing implementations
fully adopt the mailbox-as-actor design. Actor frameworks like Kilim for
Java~\cite{kilim-framework} demonstrate partial alignment by decoupling
mailboxes from actors ---treating them as object/work-queues where actors pull
messages when ready to process them. We identify analogous approaches in the
JavaScript ecosystem, such as the WeChaty library employed in the industrial chatbot
applications using actor-like architectures~\cite{wechaty}.
Our work's distinct contribution
lies in formally promoting mailboxes to first-class citizens, actors, within the
model itself, rather than treating them as implementation details or external
components.

\paragraph{Rules as way to guide implementation}
The operational semantics given in \Cref{sec:semantics} can be seen as a way to
guide the implementation of the \say{mailbox-as-an-actor} approach, described in this
paper. We presented the rules in such a way that they should be easy to reason about and
implement.

The system-level rules are given in \Cref{sec:creation-rules}. In this section,
we described how the system grows from the initial empty state, with the addition
of nodes, engines, and later on messages in \Cref{sec:message-passing-rules}. We
cover other rules such as \textsc{Clean} as a garbage-collector for engines,
however, we did not cover rules for on-demand engine or node
deletion/disconnection. This can be seen as an extension of the model.

Our message-passing mechanism implements the rules given in
\Cref{sec:message-passing-rules}. The \textsc{Send} rule enables asynchronous
message realisation in the system, allowing concurrent message generation
without sender blocking. Messages are reliably delivered to mailbox engines
through the \textsc{Enqueue} rule, which implements atomic \say{enqueuing}.
Message digestion is handled by the \textsc{Dequeue} rule.

Finally, and probably one of the most interesting rules, the \textsc{Process}
rule enables atomic state transitions by combining behaviour evaluation, rules
in \Cref{sec:behavior-eval-rules} with effect handling, and rules in
\Cref{sec:effect-rules}. Together, the rules in \Cref{sec:semantics} form a
complete system for reliable asynchronous communication with proper resource
management.

\paragraph{Future work}
There is an ongoing effort to implement this work in Elixir via macros that
generate the necessary code to support the mailbox primitive. The library is a
work-in-progress, and the code is available at
\url{https://github.com/anoma/engine}, branch \texttt{mailbox-actors}.

\section{Acknowledgements}

This work was inspired by discussions between Jeremy Ornelas and Christophe De
Troyer. Thanks to Anthony Hart and Tobias Heindel for helpful feedback and
discussions.
